words.definition: open to more than one interpretation; unclear
words.example: The politician's ambiguous answer left journalists confused.

words.definition: having mixed feelings about something
words.example: She felt ambivalent about moving to a new city.

words.definition: a short amusing or interesting story about a real incident
words.example: He began his speech with a funny anecdote about his first job.

words.definition: a person who actively opposes someone; a rival character
words.example: The novel's antagonist manipulates every character around him.

words.definition: able to express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively
words.example: She gave an articulate explanation of the experiment's results.

words.definition: a confident statement of fact or belief
words.example: The essay opens with a bold assertion about climate change.

words.definition: showing a willingness to take bold risks
words.example: The audacious plan involved crossing the border at night.

words.definition: the right or condition of self-governance; independence
words.example: Teenagers often seek greater autonomy from their parents.

words.definition: providing psychological relief through the release of strong emotion
words.example: Writing in her journal was a cathartic experience after the exam.

words.definition: logical and consistent; easy to follow
words.example: He struggled to give a coherent account of the accident.

words.definition: showing smug self-satisfaction; unaware of danger
words.example: The champions became complacent and lost the final.

words.definition: an idea or feeling a word invokes beyond its literal meaning
words.example: The word 'home' has warmer connotations than 'house'.

words.definition: causing or likely to cause an argument
words.example: Immigration remains a contentious topic in politics.

words.definition: belonging to the present time; modern
words.example: The gallery specialises in contemporary art.

words.definition: able to be believed; convincing
words.example: The witness gave a credible account of the events.

words.definition: distrustful of human sincerity or integrity
words.example: His cynical view of politicians made him refuse to vote.

words.definition: respectful submission to another's wishes or judgement
words.example: The junior staff treated the director with great deference.

words.definition: the lack or denial of something considered necessary
words.example: Years of economic deprivation left the town struggling.

words.definition: imitative of the work of another; not original
words.example: Critics dismissed the film as derivative of earlier classics.

words.definition: in low spirits from loss of hope or courage
words.example: She grew despondent after failing her driving test twice.

words.definition: to discourage someone from doing something through fear or difficulty
words.example: High fences are meant to deter trespassers.

words.definition: intended to teach, particularly with moral instruction
words.example: The fable has a clearly didactic purpose.

words.definition: to perceive or recognise something clearly
words.example: It was hard to discern any pattern in the data.

words.definition: a feeling of contempt for someone or something
words.example: He looked at the offer with obvious disdain.

words.definition: to regard or describe as being of little worth
words.example: The critic disparaged the novel as shallow and clichéd.

words.definition: inclined to lay down principles as unquestionably true
words.example: His dogmatic approach left no room for debate.

words.definition: a division or contrast between two things that are opposed or entirely different
words.example: The essay explores the dichotomy between good and evil.

words.definition: believing in equal rights and opportunities for all people
words.example: The school promotes an egalitarian approach to education.

words.definition: outstandingly bad; shocking
words.example: The referee's egregious error changed the result of the match.

words.definition: to draw out a response or reaction from someone
words.example: The teacher's question elicited a thoughtful discussion.

words.definition: fluent and persuasive speaking or writing
words.example: The lawyer's eloquence swayed the jury.

words.definition: based on observation or experience rather than theory
words.example: The claim needs empirical evidence to be taken seriously.

words.definition: to include comprehensively
words.example: The syllabus encompasses both classic and modern literature.

words.definition: difficult to interpret or understand; mysterious
words.example: The Mona Lisa's enigmatic smile has fascinated viewers for centuries.

words.definition: lasting for a very short time
words.example: Fame on social media can be remarkably ephemeral.

words.definition: a perfect example of a particular quality
words.example: She is the epitome of grace under pressure.

words.definition: understood by only a small number of people with specialist knowledge
words.example: The lecture was full of esoteric references to ancient philosophy.

words.definition: a mild expression substituted for a harsh or blunt one
words.example: 'Passed away' is a common euphemism for 'died'.

words.definition: to make a problem or situation worse
words.example: Cutting funding will only exacerbate the housing crisis.

words.definition: to be a typical example of; to illustrate
words.example: Her actions exemplify true leadership.

words.definition: to make use of, often unfairly, for one's own advantage
words.example: The factory was accused of exploiting its workers.

words.definition: to extend conclusions based on known facts to an unknown situation
words.example: Scientists extrapolated future sea levels from current data.

words.definition: a mistaken belief based on unsound reasoning
words.example: It's a common fallacy that reading in dim light damages your eyes.

words.definition: very attentive to detail; hard to please
words.example: He was fastidious about keeping his desk perfectly tidy.

words.definition: inspiring fear or respect through being impressively powerful
words.example: The debate champion was a formidable opponent.

words.definition: dominance of one group, class, or country over others
words.example: The essay examines cultural hegemony in global media.

words.definition: exaggerated statements not meant to be taken literally
words.example: 'I've told you a million times' is an example of hyperbole.

words.definition: a proposed explanation made on limited evidence, to be tested further
words.example: The scientist tested her hypothesis with a controlled experiment.

words.definition: a person who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions
words.example: The artist was seen as an iconoclast who rejected classical rules.

words.definition: a distinctive habit or way of behaving peculiar to a person
words.example: One of his idiosyncrasies is reading the last page of a book first.

words.definition: a system of ideas and ideals forming the basis of a theory or policy
words.example: The two parties differ sharply in political ideology.

words.definition: about to happen very soon
words.example: Dark clouds signalled an imminent storm.

words.definition: to involve oneself deeply in an activity
words.example: She immersed herself in French culture during her exchange year.

words.definition: treating all sides or rivals equally; not biased
words.example: A judge must remain impartial throughout the trial.

words.definition: implied though not plainly expressed
words.example: There was an implicit warning in her tone of voice.

words.definition: a conclusion that can be drawn though not explicitly stated
words.example: The implication of his silence was that he disagreed.

words.definition: certain to happen; unavoidable
words.example: Given the delays, a postponement seemed inevitable.

words.definition: a conclusion reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning
words.example: From the wet ground, we made the inference that it had rained.

words.definition: innocent and unsuspecting; naive
words.example: His ingenuous question revealed how little he knew of politics.

words.definition: inborn; natural rather than learned
words.example: Some argue that a sense of fairness is innate in humans.

words.definition: proceeding in a gradual, subtle way but with harmful effects
words.example: Stress can have an insidious effect on health over time.

words.definition: necessary to make a whole complete; essential
words.example: Practice is integral to mastering any instrument.

words.definition: belonging naturally; essential to the character of something
words.example: The painting's intrinsic value lies in its craftsmanship, not its price.

words.definition: to cite or appeal to as an authority; to call upon
words.example: The lawyer invoked an old law to support her case.

words.definition: a contrast between expectation and reality, often humorous
words.example: It was pure irony that the fire station burned down.

words.definition: to place things side by side for contrasting effect
words.example: The film juxtaposes scenes of war with scenes of peace.

words.definition: to express sorrow or regret for something
words.example: The poem laments the loss of a simpler way of life.

words.definition: conforming to the law or to rules; genuine
words.example: She raised a legitimate concern about the budget.

words.definition: expressed clearly; easy to understand
words.example: His lucid explanation made a complex topic easy to follow.

words.definition: a general feeling of discomfort or unease
words.example: A sense of malaise spread through the struggling economy.

words.definition: to control or influence cleverly or unscrupulously
words.example: The advert tries to manipulate viewers into buying more.

words.definition: to treat a person or group as insignificant or peripheral
words.example: The policy risks marginalising rural communities.

words.definition: showing great attention to detail; very careful
words.example: The historian was meticulous in checking every source.

words.definition: a recurring element or theme in an artistic work
words.example: Water is a recurring motif throughout the novel.

words.definition: an extremely large or countless number of
words.example: The festival offered a myriad of activities for visitors.

words.definition: the belief that life is meaningless and traditional values are baseless
words.example: The play explores nihilism through its bleak, purposeless characters.

words.definition: feeling sentimental longing for the past
words.example: The old photographs made her feel nostalgic for childhood summers.

words.definition: a conception, belief, or idea
words.example: He dismissed the notion that money brings happiness.

words.definition: a subtle difference in meaning, expression, or tone
words.example: A good translator captures every nuance of the original text.

words.definition: not influenced by personal feelings; based on facts
words.example: Journalists should try to remain objective when reporting news.

words.definition: not aware of or not noticing something
words.example: He was oblivious to the whispers behind his back.

words.definition: no longer produced or used; out of date
words.example: Fax machines have become almost entirely obsolete.

words.definition: giving the impression that something bad is going to happen
words.example: An ominous silence fell over the courtroom.

words.definition: a statement that contradicts itself but may nonetheless be true
words.example: It's a paradox that the more choices we have, the harder deciding becomes.

words.definition: a typical example or pattern of something; a model
words.example: The discovery created a new paradigm in modern physics.

words.definition: a quality in speech or writing that evokes pity or sadness
words.example: The film's final scene is filled with quiet pathos.

words.definition: carried out with minimum effort or reflection
words.example: He gave a perfunctory nod without really listening.

words.definition: spreading widely throughout an area or group of people
words.example: Social media's influence has become pervasive in modern life.

words.definition: seeming reasonable or probable
words.example: She offered a plausible explanation for her absence.

words.definition: to divide into two sharply contrasting groups or opinions
words.example: The proposal has polarised public opinion.

words.definition: dealing with things sensibly and realistically
words.example: The government took a pragmatic approach to the crisis.

words.definition: not securely held or in a dangerous, uncertain state
words.example: The ladder was balanced in a precarious position.

words.definition: widespread in a particular area or at a particular time
words.example: Poverty remains prevalent in many parts of the world.

words.definition: very great or intense; showing deep insight
words.example: The book had a profound effect on how she saw the world.

words.definition: biased or misleading information used to promote a political cause
words.example: The regime used propaganda to control public opinion.

words.definition: written or spoken language in its ordinary form, without metrical structure
words.example: The novelist is known for his elegant, flowing prose.

words.definition: representing the most perfect or typical example of a quality
words.example: The seaside town is the quintessential English holiday spot.

words.definition: the art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing
words.example: The candidate's rhetoric appealed strongly to young voters.

words.definition: able to recover quickly from difficulties; tough
words.example: Children are often remarkably resilient after setbacks.

words.definition: relating to persuasive language, especially a question asked for effect
words.example: 'Who doesn't love a holiday?' is a rhetorical question.

words.definition: the use of humour or exaggeration to criticise people's vices or follies
words.example: The show is a sharp satire of modern politics.

words.definition: to examine closely and critically
words.example: Auditors scrutinised the company's accounts for errors.

words.definition: based on personal feelings or opinions rather than facts
words.example: Taste in music is entirely subjective.

words.definition: existing only on the surface; lacking depth
words.example: The review offered only a superficial analysis of the film.

words.definition: perceptible by touch; clear and definite
words.example: The negotiations produced no tangible results.

words.definition: the ability to make good judgements and quick decisions
words.example: Her business acumen turned a market stall into a national chain.

words.definition: very skilled or proficient at something
words.example: He became adept at defusing arguments between his friends.

words.definition: to warn or reprimand someone firmly
words.example: The teacher admonished the class for talking during the exam.

words.definition: difficulties or misfortune
words.example: The team showed great character in the face of adversity.

words.definition: to publicly recommend or support
words.example: The charity advocates free school meals for all children.

words.definition: having a great deal of money; wealthy
words.example: The affluent suburb was lined with large detached houses.

words.definition: to make someone feel isolated or estranged
words.example: His arrogance began to alienate even his closest supporters.

words.definition: to claim something is true without proof
words.example: The report alleges that safety checks were skipped.

words.definition: to make suffering or a problem less severe
words.example: The new bypass should alleviate congestion in the town centre.

words.definition: distant and not friendly
words.example: She seemed aloof at first, but was simply shy.

words.definition: showing selfless concern for the wellbeing of others
words.example: His altruistic decision to donate a kidney saved a stranger's life.

words.definition: to make something bad better
words.example: Investment in housing helped ameliorate conditions in the area.

words.definition: comparable in certain respects
words.example: The heart's function is analogous to that of a pump.

words.definition: strong hostility or hatred
words.example: Years of rivalry created deep animosity between the two clubs.

words.definition: the direct opposite of something
words.example: Her calm approach was the antithesis of his fiery temper.

words.definition: lack of interest, enthusiasm, or concern
words.example: Voter apathy led to the lowest turnout in decades.

words.definition: to pacify someone by giving in to their demands
words.example: He offered to wash up to appease his annoyed flatmate.

words.definition: based on random choice rather than reason or system
words.example: The curfew time seemed entirely arbitrary to the students.

words.definition: very old or old-fashioned
words.example: The law relies on archaic language that few now understand.

words.definition: very enthusiastic or passionate
words.example: She is an ardent supporter of women's football.

words.definition: to find out something for certain
words.example: Police are trying to ascertain the cause of the fire.

words.definition: to absorb and integrate ideas or people
words.example: It took weeks to assimilate all the information from the course.

words.definition: having the ability to accurately assess situations and turn them to advantage
words.example: An astute investor spotted the company's potential early.

words.definition: an extremely wicked or cruel act
words.example: The documentary examined atrocities committed during the war.

words.definition: to make something greater by adding to it
words.example: She tutored at weekends to augment her income.

words.definition: severe or strict in appearance or manner; without comforts
words.example: The monastery's austere rooms contained only a bed and a desk.

words.definition: extreme greed for wealth or material gain
words.example: The banker's avarice blinded him to the risks he was taking.

words.definition: a strong dislike or disinclination
words.example: She has a lifelong aversion to public speaking.

words.definition: hostile and aggressive
words.example: The belligerent fan was escorted out of the stadium.

words.definition: gentle and kind; harmless
words.example: His benign smile put the nervous interviewees at ease.

words.definition: to support or strengthen
words.example: The win bolstered the team's confidence before the final.

words.definition: concise and exact use of words; shortness of time
words.example: The speech was admired for its brevity and wit.

words.definition: overly complicated administrative procedure
words.example: Endless bureaucracy delayed the charity's relief work.

words.definition: showing a cruel disregard for others' feelings
words.example: His callous remark about her failure upset everyone.

words.definition: the quality of being open and honest
words.example: She discussed her mistakes with surprising candour.

words.definition: to give in to an opponent or demand
words.example: The council capitulated to public pressure and reopened the library.

words.definition: given to sudden changes of mood or behaviour
words.example: The capricious weather ruined three picnics in a row.

words.definition: a person or thing that causes a change or event
words.example: The pamphlet became a catalyst for political reform.

words.definition: sarcastic in a scathing, bitter way
words.example: The critic's caustic review left the chef furious.

words.definition: to express severe disapproval formally
words.example: The MP was censured for misleading Parliament.

words.definition: kept secret, especially because illicit
words.example: The rebels held clandestine meetings in the cellar.

words.definition: to persuade someone by using force or threats
words.example: No one should be coerced into signing a contract.

words.definition: forming a united, well-connected whole
words.example: The essay needs a more cohesive argument to earn top marks.

words.definition: to honour the memory of a person or event
words.example: A plaque commemorates the poet's birthplace.

words.definition: involved with others in wrongdoing
words.example: By staying silent, the witnesses were complicit in the cover-up.

words.definition: to admit something is true after first denying it; to surrender
words.example: He finally conceded that his rival's plan was better.

words.definition: intended to placate or reconcile
words.example: Her conciliatory tone helped end the dispute.

words.definition: to agree with someone or something
words.example: The second doctor concurred with the original diagnosis.

words.definition: to accept or allow behaviour that is wrong
words.example: The school does not condone bullying of any kind.

words.definition: making a certain outcome likely or possible
words.example: A quiet room is conducive to effective revision.

words.definition: an opinion formed without complete evidence; a guess
words.example: Reports of the manager's exit remain pure conjecture.

words.definition: to secretly plot or allow wrongdoing
words.example: The guards connived with the smugglers to move the goods.

words.definition: general agreement among a group
words.example: After hours of debate, the committee reached a consensus.

words.definition: to comfort someone at a time of grief or disappointment
words.example: Teammates consoled the goalkeeper after the penalty miss.

words.definition: a component part of something; a voter in an area
words.example: Hydrogen is a constituent of water.

words.definition: to interpret a word or action in a particular way
words.example: Her silence could be construed as agreement.

words.definition: abundant in supply or quantity
words.example: He took copious notes throughout the lecture.

words.definition: to confirm or give support to a statement or finding
words.example: CCTV footage corroborated the witness's account.

words.definition: not openly acknowledged or displayed; secret
words.example: The agency ran a covert operation for two years.

words.definition: too ready to believe things; gullible
words.example: Credulous readers fell for the obviously fake story.

words.definition: having a meaning that is mysterious or obscure
words.example: She left only a cryptic note saying 'gone to find the answer'.

words.definition: to reach a climax or final point
words.example: The festival culminated in a spectacular fireworks display.

words.definition: deserving blame
words.example: The inquiry found the contractors culpable for the collapse.

words.definition: hasty and therefore not thorough
words.example: A cursory glance at the figures missed the obvious error.

words.definition: to make someone or something very weak
words.example: The long illness debilitated the once-athletic runner.

words.definition: behaviour in keeping with good taste and propriety
words.example: The ceremony was conducted with great decorum.

words.definition: outward behaviour or bearing
words.example: Her calm demeanour hid her racing nerves.

words.definition: to publicly declare something to be wrong or evil
words.example: World leaders denounced the invasion within hours.

words.definition: to use up the supply of something
words.example: Overfishing has depleted stocks in the North Sea.

words.definition: to feel or express strong disapproval of
words.example: She deplored the waste of food at the banquet.

words.definition: showing a critical or disrespectful attitude
words.example: He was suspended for making derogatory comments online.

words.definition: empty and bleak; feeling abandoned and miserable
words.example: The desolate moor stretched grey to the horizon.

words.definition: extremely poor and lacking the means to provide for oneself
words.example: The flood left hundreds of families destitute.

words.definition: to depart from an established course or standard
words.example: The pilot deviated from the flight path to avoid the storm.

words.definition: to leave the main subject temporarily in speech or writing
words.example: The lecturer digressed into a story about his student days.

words.definition: a lack of compatibility between facts or claims
words.example: Auditors found a discrepancy between the two sets of accounts.

words.definition: a great difference, often seen as unfair
words.example: The report highlighted the disparity in funding between regions.

words.definition: to spread information widely
words.example: The findings were disseminated to schools across the country.

words.definition: the holding or expression of opinions at variance with those officially held
words.example: The new policy provoked open dissent among staff.

words.definition: drawing on a wide and diverse range of sources
words.example: Her eclectic playlist ranged from opera to drum and bass.

words.definition: the ability to produce a desired result; effectiveness
words.example: Trials will test the efficacy of the new vaccine.

words.definition: difficult to find, catch, or achieve
words.example: A good night's sleep proved elusive before the exam.

words.definition: to match or surpass someone by imitation
words.example: Young players hope to emulate their sporting heroes.

words.definition: to cause or give rise to a feeling or situation
words.example: Openness engenders trust within a team.

words.definition: a state of active opposition or hatred
words.example: Decades of enmity divided the two families.

words.definition: open to more than one interpretation; deliberately vague
words.example: His equivocal answer satisfied neither side of the debate.

words.definition: to destroy or get rid of completely
words.example: The campaign aims to eradicate polio worldwide.

words.definition: serving as a desirable model; outstandingly good
words.example: Her exemplary attendance record earned a special award.

words.definition: to officially clear someone of blame
words.example: New evidence exonerated the man after ten years in prison.

words.definition: to make an action or process happen more quickly
words.example: The embassy agreed to expedite her visa application.

words.definition: stated clearly and in detail, leaving no room for doubt
words.example: The instructions were explicit: no phones in the exam hall.

words.definition: the front of a building; a deceptive outward appearance
words.example: Behind his cheerful facade, he was deeply worried.

words.definition: treating serious issues with inappropriate humour
words.example: His facetious remark fell flat at the sombre meeting.

words.definition: having or displaying passionate intensity
words.example: She is a fervent believer in equal access to education.

words.definition: conspicuously offensive; blatant
words.example: The handball was a flagrant breach of the rules.

words.definition: happening by lucky chance
words.example: A fortuitous meeting at the station led to her first job.

words.definition: not having any serious purpose or value
words.example: The judge dismissed the claim as frivolous.

words.definition: to shock or excite someone into taking action
words.example: The defeat galvanized the squad into training harder.

words.definition: uncalled for; done without good reason
words.example: Critics objected to the film's gratuitous violence.

words.definition: fond of company; sociable
words.example: Her gregarious nature made her the heart of every gathering.

words.definition: overused and consequently lacking originality
words.example: The essay opened with the hackneyed phrase 'since the dawn of time'.

words.definition: to delay or block the progress of something
words.example: Heavy snow impeded the rescue effort.

words.definition: continuing without pause or interruption
words.example: The incessant drilling next door made revision impossible.

words.definition: having no particular interest or sympathy; unconcerned
words.example: He seemed indifferent to the praise his work received.

words.definition: feeling or showing anger at unfair treatment
words.example: She was indignant at being blamed for someone else's mistake.
